blurb: Aromatic Mediterranean evergreen herb; needle foliage and tiny blue winter flowers bees work — pot it and bring it in over winter.
size: 2–4 ft × 2–4 ft in a large container; wild plants reach 4–5 ft
water: Low; let it dry between waterings — pots dry fast, check often
spread: Clumping; upright or mounding depending on cultivar, not spreading
seasons: Evergreen silver-green needle foliage year-round; pale blue flowers from late winter through spring indoors, and again in spring after it goes back out
wildlife: Bees and butterflies visit the flowers; a valuable late-winter nectar source when blooming indoors
toxic: Non-toxic to dogs, cats, and horses
origin: Mediterranean basin — S. Europe, N. Africa, and the Middle East
habitat: Dry rocky coastal scrub and garrigue on thin limestone soils

care.hardiness: Rosemary (*Salvia rosmarinus*) is rated USDA zones 8–10 outdoors, meaning it is killed by hard Front-Range winters — Boulder sits firmly in zone 5b–6a. Treat it as a tender perennial: grow it in a container year-round and bring it inside before the first hard frost (usually early-to-mid October). The hardiest cultivar readily available in Colorado is 'Arp', which can occasionally survive zone 7 winters in a very protected spot, but even 'Arp' cannot be relied upon to overwinter in the ground on the Front Range.[1][2]
care.planting: Grow rosemary entirely in a container — terracotta or a large plastic pot at least 12 inches wide and deep, with one or more drainage holes. Use a fast-draining potting mix (cactus/herb mix or regular potting soil cut 1:1 with coarse perlite). Set the pot outside after the danger of hard frost has passed, typically mid-May on the Front Range, in a spot that gets full sun all day. Bring it back inside before the first frost in early to mid October. Indoors, rosemary prefers a bright, cool spot — a south-facing window at 50–65°F is ideal; it will often bloom through winter and early spring in these conditions, providing nectar for any bees active on warm days. Do not let it sit in a saucer of water — root rot from waterlogged soil is the most common cause of death.[1][2]
care.sun: Full sun is essential — 6 or more hours of direct sunlight daily. Indoors, a south-facing window supplemented with a grow light during the short days of December and January helps keep the plant healthy. Poor indoor light causes leggy, weak growth and is a slow way to lose the plant over winter.[1][2]
care.soil: Lean, fast-draining soil with excellent aeration. Rosemary evolved on thin, rocky Mediterranean limestone; it does not want rich potting compost. A cactus/succulent mix or a standard potting soil amended with at least 25–30% coarse perlite or horticultural grit gives the drainage and airflow the roots need. Never use soil that stays wet for more than a day or two after watering.[1][2]
care.water: Water thoroughly, then allow the top inch or two of soil to dry before watering again — roughly every 5–10 days in summer, every 10–14 days indoors in winter depending on pot size, light, and temperature. Containers dry out faster than ground beds in Boulder's dry, sunny summers, so check more often than you think you need to. Overwatering is the number-one killer of rosemary; when in doubt, skip a watering rather than give one.[1][2]
care.spacing: Allow at least 2–3 ft of clear air around the pot. Rosemary needs good airflow to prevent powdery mildew, a common problem when it is crowded or kept in still indoor air through winter. A small fan set on low nearby can help in tight indoor spaces.[2]
care.propagation: Rosemary is reliably propagated from stem cuttings and rarely from seed (which is very slow). Take 4–6 inch softwood or semi-hardwood cuttings in late spring through mid-summer; strip the lower leaves, dip the cut end in rooting hormone powder, and insert into a very free-draining mix of perlite and a little compost. Keep the cuttings in bright indirect light and lightly moist; roots form in 3–6 weeks. Layering — pinning a low branch to the soil surface until it roots, then severing it — is another reliable non-seed method.[1][2]
care.bloom: Rosemary blooms from late winter into spring — often beginning in February or March when grown in a bright, cool indoor spot, and continuing after it goes back outside in May. The small two-lipped flowers range from pale lavender-blue to deeper blue or white depending on cultivar. They appear along the stems in clusters among the needle-like leaves.[1][2]
care.feeding: Little to none. A very dilute balanced fertilizer (e.g. half-strength 10-10-10) applied once in spring when the plant goes back outside is plenty. Do not fertilize through the winter or you'll push weak, pale growth in low indoor light.[2]
care.maintenance: Prune lightly in spring after bloom — trim back by up to one-third to encourage bushy new growth and keep the plant compact for the container. Avoid cutting into bare old wood, which may not resprout. Pinching back new stem tips through the growing season keeps the plant full. Remove any dead wood when repotting in spring (every 2–3 years, moving up one pot size).[1][2]
care.troubles: Powdery mildew and root rot are the main problems. Both are prevented by good airflow around the plant and fast-draining soil. Indoors watch for spider mites and mealybugs, especially in low-humidity winter rooms; wipe stems with rubbing alcohol or use insecticidal soap. Overwintering failure is almost always caused by root rot (overwatering in dim light) or by insufficient light causing a slow decline.[2]

edible.summary: One of the most widely used culinary herbs: the fresh or dried leaves flavour roasted meat, bread, soups, and vegetables; the flowers are milder and can be used as a garnish.[1][3]
edible.parts: The fresh or dried needle-like leaves are the primary culinary part — harvest young stem tips for the best flavour. The small pale-blue flowers are also edible and carry a milder rosemary flavour; they can be sprinkled over salads, pasta, or savoury dishes as a garnish.[3][2]
edible.preparation: Strip leaves from the stem or use whole sprigs for roasting and slow cooking; remove whole sprigs before serving. Chop finely for bread doughs, marinades, and herb butter. Use the flowers fresh only — they do not dry well and are best used immediately as a garnish or infused briefly into syrups or honey.[3]
edible.caution: Rosemary is safe in normal culinary quantities and is non-toxic to dogs, cats, and horses.[4] However, concentrated rosemary essential oil — which is far more potent than the herb — should never be taken internally, and medicinal doses of rosemary extract have been associated with adverse effects including seizures at very high levels. People who are pregnant are traditionally advised to avoid rosemary in quantities beyond normal food seasoning. For kitchen use with fresh or dried herb at ordinary culinary amounts, no caution is needed. Harvest only from plants you know have not been treated with systemic pesticides.[3]
